slides.lead: This is a demonstration (sample) template. All text is dummy content and exists only to show off the styles.
slides.notes: Opening slide. Put the event title, a one-line subtitle and a meta row at the bottom here. Wrap one word in em for the accent color.
Team slide. Instead of photos it uses initials in a colored circle, so it needs no images.

slides.lead: Three parts, with room for questions between them.
slides.notes: Agenda as three large tiles. Good at the start of a block.

slides.lead: Our exposure, the data, and the biggest gaps.
slides.notes: Section divider between blocks. Large heading on a colored gradient.

slides.lead: Identities, devices and cloud apps blur every old boundary. Trust now has to be earned at every request.
slides.footnote: // illustrative slide with an enlarged image
slides.notes: Hero slide: large heading, a strong illustration, short caption. Good for a key point or a chapter.

items: average time to detect an intrusion left unmonitored
fewer phishing clicks after targeted training
of breaches blocked by multi-factor authentication
bullets: Measure dwell time, not alert volume: speed of detection wins.
Start where the crown jewels and the real pain actually sit.
A small pilot with a clear metric beats a big plan without one.
slides.notes: KPI dashboard: three cards with an icon, a big number and a caption, with supporting bullets below.

bullets: Every identity and device on each request.
Least privilege, only what the task needs.
Contain blast radius if one host falls.
Telemetry on everything, all the time.
slides.notes: Two-column slide: bullets with a strong lead word on the left, illustration on the right. Text scales itself.

slides.lead: Same threat, two approaches and two cost curves.
cards: We respond once the breach is public. Costly and chaotic.
We pass the audit checklist. Better, but often only on paper.
We hunt and patch before exploitation. Lowest risk.
We assume breach and rehearse recovery until it is routine.
slides.notes: A 2x2 grid of cards. Good for comparisons or listing variants.

slides.lead: Six typical building blocks of a modern defense.
cards: Strong MFA and single sign-on at the front door.
Live view of endpoints, logs and anomalies.
Flags unusual behavior before a human would.
Tamper-proof records for audits and forensics.
Devices hardened, isolated and always patched.
A team that spots and reports the lures.
slides.notes: A 3x2 grid with an icon in each card header. Great for an overview of tools or capabilities.

items: Familiar attacks keep coming, basics still cover most of them.
Tooling scales fast, exploits land within hours of disclosure.
Attackers reason about defenses and rewrite their own playbook.
slides.notes: Numbered cards with small trend charts and an illustration on the right. Ideal for three possible futures.

slides.lead: Five steps, each with a clear output.
slides.notes: A horizontal flow of steps with arrows. Each box has a heading and a small caption.

slides.quote: It is not the firm with the most tools that stays safe, but the one that responds the fastest.
slides.by: Sample quote to show the style
slides.notes: Quote slide with a bold left rule. Keep the attribution small.

slides.lead: Simple, but easy to forget under pressure.
bullets: Every alert has a name attached and a clear handoff.
You know up front how success is defined.
Drill the playbook before the real incident hits.
slides.notes: A plain bullet slide with a strong lead word. Text scales automatically to fill the space.

slides.lead: A playbook nobody rehearsed fails on the day it matters.
slides.notes: Statement slide: one large sentence centered on the page. Use it for the key message.

slides.lead: What to do this week, this month and this quarter.
slides.notes: Second divider, opening the practical part.

slides.h1: Stop a phishing attack in 30 seconds.
slides.lead: We switch into the console and quarantine a live threat together.
slides.footnote: // placeholder slide for a demo or a break
slides.notes: Placeholder slide. Centered heading, ideal for a live demo, a break or questions.
Closing slide styled like the title. Contact details and a call to action.
